title: Broadband Technologies
description: Practice identifying broadband Internet access technologies including DSL variants (ADSL, VDSL, SDSL), DOCSIS cable modem standards, fiber-to-the-premises (FTTP/FTTH), and comparing their speeds, distances, and use cases.
starterCode:
# BROADBAND TECHNOLOGIES EXERCISE

## Part 1: DSL Fundamentals

1. DSL stands for Digital Subscriber _______.
2. DSL transmits data over existing _______ telephone lines (twisted-pair copper).
3. A DSL _______ is the device at the customer premises that modulates/demodulates the DSL signal.
4. At the provider's central office, a _______ (Digital Subscriber Line Access Multiplexer) aggregates multiple DSL connections.
5. DSL performance degrades as the distance from the _______ increases, typically limited to about 18,000 feet.

## Part 2: DSL Variants

6. ADSL stands for _______ Digital Subscriber Line, offering different upload and download speeds.
7. ADSL provides faster _______ speeds than upload speeds, making it ideal for home Internet use.
8. ADSL supports maximum download speeds of approximately _______ Mbps over short distances.
9. SDSL stands for _______ Digital Subscriber Line, providing equal upload and download speeds.
10. SDSL is commonly used by _______ that need to host servers or upload large files regularly.
11. VDSL stands for Very High Bit Rate Digital Subscriber Line and can achieve speeds up to _______ Mbps.
12. VDSL has a much shorter maximum distance of about _______ feet compared to ADSL.
13. VDSL2 is an enhanced version supporting speeds up to _______ Mbps over very short loops.

## Part 3: Cable Modem and DOCSIS

14. Cable Internet uses the same _______ cable infrastructure originally designed for television.
15. DOCSIS stands for Data Over Cable Service Interface _______.
16. DOCSIS 3.0 supports maximum download speeds of approximately _______ Mbps by bonding multiple channels.
17. DOCSIS 3.1 increased maximum download speeds to approximately _______ Gbps.
18. Cable Internet is a _______ medium, meaning bandwidth is shared among users in the same neighborhood.
19. The cable modem connects to the provider's _______ (Cable Modem Termination System) at the headend.
20. During peak usage times, cable Internet speeds may decrease due to _______ from other subscribers.

## Part 4: Fiber Technologies (FTTP/FTTH)

21. FTTH stands for Fiber to the _______.
22. FTTP stands for Fiber to the _______.
23. Fiber optic connections use pulses of _______ to transmit data instead of electrical signals.
24. PON stands for _______ Optical Network, a common FTTH architecture.
25. An OLT (Optical Line _______) is the provider-side device that manages the PON network.
26. An ONT (Optical Network _______) is the customer-premises device that converts optical to electrical signals.
27. GPON supports download speeds of up to _______ Gbps and upload speeds of about 1.25 Gbps.
28. Fiber offers significantly lower _______ compared to DSL and cable, often under 5 milliseconds.

## Part 5: Broadband Comparison

29. For symmetric upload/download needs in a business, _______ DSL is the most appropriate DSL variant.
30. The broadband technology offering the highest speeds and lowest latency is _______.
31. In rural areas with no cable or fiber, _______ may be the only wired broadband option using phone lines.
32. Cable Internet's biggest disadvantage compared to fiber is _______ bandwidth with neighbors.
33. The maximum practical distance for ADSL service from the central office is approximately _______ miles (about 18,000 feet).
solution:
# SOLUTION

## Part 1: DSL Fundamentals

1. DSL stands for Digital Subscriber **Line**.
2. DSL transmits data over existing **copper** telephone lines (twisted-pair copper).
3. A DSL **modem** is the device at the customer premises that modulates/demodulates the DSL signal.
4. At the provider's central office, a **DSLAM** (Digital Subscriber Line Access Multiplexer) aggregates multiple DSL connections.
5. DSL performance degrades as the distance from the **central office** increases, typically limited to about 18,000 feet.

## Part 2: DSL Variants

6. ADSL stands for **Asymmetric** Digital Subscriber Line, offering different upload and download speeds.
7. ADSL provides faster **download** speeds than upload speeds, making it ideal for home Internet use.
8. ADSL supports maximum download speeds of approximately **24** Mbps over short distances.
9. SDSL stands for **Symmetric** Digital Subscriber Line, providing equal upload and download speeds.
10. SDSL is commonly used by **businesses** that need to host servers or upload large files regularly.
11. VDSL stands for Very High Bit Rate Digital Subscriber Line and can achieve speeds up to **52** Mbps.
12. VDSL has a much shorter maximum distance of about **4,000** feet compared to ADSL.
13. VDSL2 is an enhanced version supporting speeds up to **100** Mbps over very short loops.

## Part 3: Cable Modem and DOCSIS

14. Cable Internet uses the same **coaxial** cable infrastructure originally designed for television.
15. DOCSIS stands for Data Over Cable Service Interface **Specification**.
16. DOCSIS 3.0 supports maximum download speeds of approximately **1,000** Mbps by bonding multiple channels.
17. DOCSIS 3.1 increased maximum download speeds to approximately **10** Gbps.
18. Cable Internet is a **shared** medium, meaning bandwidth is shared among users in the same neighborhood.
19. The cable modem connects to the provider's **CMTS** (Cable Modem Termination System) at the headend.
20. During peak usage times, cable Internet speeds may decrease due to **congestion** from other subscribers.

## Part 4: Fiber Technologies (FTTP/FTTH)

21. FTTH stands for Fiber to the **Home**.
22. FTTP stands for Fiber to the **Premises**.
23. Fiber optic connections use pulses of **light** to transmit data instead of electrical signals.
24. PON stands for **Passive** Optical Network, a common FTTH architecture.
25. An OLT (Optical Line **Terminal**) is the provider-side device that manages the PON network.
26. An ONT (Optical Network **Terminal**) is the customer-premises device that converts optical to electrical signals.
27. GPON supports download speeds of up to **2.488** Gbps and upload speeds of about 1.25 Gbps.
28. Fiber offers significantly lower **latency** compared to DSL and cable, often under 5 milliseconds.

## Part 5: Broadband Comparison

29. For symmetric upload/download needs in a business, **SDSL** DSL is the most appropriate DSL variant.
30. The broadband technology offering the highest speeds and lowest latency is **fiber**.
31. In rural areas with no cable or fiber, **DSL** may be the only wired broadband option using phone lines.
32. Cable Internet's biggest disadvantage compared to fiber is **shared** bandwidth with neighbors.
33. The maximum practical distance for ADSL service from the central office is approximately **3.4** miles (about 18,000 feet).
hints: DSL uses existing copper telephone lines; a DSLAM at the central office aggregates all DSL connections.
ADSL = Asymmetric (different upload/download speeds); SDSL = Symmetric (equal speeds); VDSL = Very high bit rate.
ADSL is best for residential (more download); SDSL is best for businesses needing equal upload/download.
VDSL offers higher speeds than ADSL but over much shorter distances (about 4,000 feet max).
DOCSIS (Data Over Cable Service Interface Specification) is the standard for cable Internet over coaxial cable.
DOCSIS 3.0 supports ~1 Gbps download; DOCSIS 3.1 supports up to 10 Gbps download speeds.
Cable Internet is a shared medium — bandwidth is split among neighborhood users, causing congestion at peak times.
FTTH/FTTP uses fiber optic cables carrying light pulses; PON (Passive Optical Network) is a common architecture.
OLT (provider side) and ONT (customer side) are the key devices in a PON/FTTH deployment.
Fiber offers the highest speeds (multi-Gbps) and lowest latency (<5ms) of all broadband technologies.
